title: CRS 001 — Generative AI Landscape
subtitle: Why the stack exists: models, prompts, LangChain, LCEL, and delivery

nodes.label: Discriminative vs Generative
nodes.definition: Discriminative AI classifies or predicts; generative AI creates new content that fits learned patterns and instructions.
nodes.whyItMatters: The course builds generative applications — you need the mental model before touching LangChain.
nodes.safeSentence: Discriminative decides a category; generative drafts new text or structured output from a prompt.
nodes.commonTrap: Treating every LLM task as classification, or assuming generative models never use probability.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Discriminative models learn boundaries between classes; generative models learn to produce plausible new outputs conditioned on input.

nodes.label: Foundation Model
nodes.definition: A broadly trained model adapted or prompted for many downstream tasks instead of one fixed label set.
nodes.whyItMatters: Enterprise value: one capable model can support summarization, Q&A, drafting, and extraction with prompting.
nodes.safeSentence: A foundation model is the shared base; prompts and app code steer it for each task.
nodes.commonTrap: Assuming one prompt works for every task without evaluation or constraints.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Foundation models are pretrained broadly and then steered with prompts, tools, or light adaptation for specific applications.

nodes.label: Large Language Model
nodes.definition: A language-focused foundation model trained to process and generate text.
nodes.whyItMatters: CRS 001 centers on LLM-backed apps — templates, chains, and parsers all assume text in / text out.
nodes.safeSentence: An LLM is the language specialist in the foundation-model family.
nodes.interviewAnswer: LLMs are foundation models specialized for language understanding and generation.

nodes.label: Prompt Engineering
nodes.definition: Designing instruction, context, examples, constraints, and output format so the model behaves reliably.
nodes.whyItMatters: Model capability alone does not make an app dependable — prompt design is the control surface.
nodes.safeSentence: Strong prompts specify what to do, what context matters, and what shape the answer should take.
nodes.commonTrap: One giant prompt with no structure, no examples, and no output contract.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Prompt engineering shapes model behavior through instructions, context, examples, and explicit output requirements.

nodes.label: Prompt Templates
nodes.definition: Reusable prompt structures with runtime variables — PromptTemplate and ChatPromptTemplate in LangChain.
nodes.whyItMatters: Templates turn ad-hoc strings into maintainable, testable application components.
nodes.safeSentence: Templates separate stable instructions from changing user data.
nodes.commonTrap: Calling the model when you only needed prompt.invoke() to fill variables.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Prompt templates encode reusable structure; invocation fills variables without necessarily calling the LLM.

nodes.label: LangChain
nodes.definition: An application framework for composing prompts, models, parsers, retrievers, tools, and conversation state.
nodes.whyItMatters: It is the glue layer between raw model calls and a maintainable generative AI application.
nodes.safeSentence: LangChain organizes prompts, models, and parsers as components you can wire together.
nodes.interviewAnswer: LangChain provides composable components and patterns for building LLM applications beyond a single API call.

nodes.label: LCEL
nodes.definition: LangChain Expression Language — pipe-style composition of runnable steps such as prompt | model | parser.
nodes.whyItMatters: LCEL is the main code pattern for reusable chains in this course.
nodes.safeSentence: LCEL connects runnables; chain.invoke() runs the full pipeline.
nodes.commonTrap: Mixing up prompt.invoke() (prepare) with chain.invoke() (run everything).
nodes.interviewAnswer: LCEL lets teams declare pipelines as connected runnables with a consistent invoke interface.

nodes.label: Structured Output
nodes.definition: Returning predictable fields and types — JSON parsers, Pydantic models, provider-native schemas.
nodes.whyItMatters: Applications need validated data, not only free-form text.
nodes.safeSentence: Structured output turns LLM responses into objects your code can trust and test.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I use parsers or provider-native structured output when downstream code needs reliable fields and types.

nodes.label: Flask Application
nodes.definition: HTTP routes and handlers that expose chains to users — prototype to production interface.
nodes.whyItMatters: Module 3 closes the loop from notebook patterns to a deliverable web application.
nodes.safeSentence: Flask wraps the chain behind routes, request validation, and HTTP responses.
nodes.commonTrap: Putting business logic only in routes with no reusable chain layer.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I keep the LangChain pipeline reusable and let Flask handle HTTP concerns and validation.

nodes.label: Evaluation and Governance
nodes.definition: Model selection, evaluation metrics, monitoring, and refinement after deployment.
nodes.whyItMatters: Production generative AI needs measurement — not only a working demo.
nodes.safeSentence: Evaluate outputs, monitor behavior, and refine prompts and models over time.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I pair functional delivery with evaluation criteria and operational monitoring for generative features.

paths.label: Stack Lane
paths.description: Follow the course arc from model concept to delivered application.

paths.label: Interview Lane
paths.description: Short spoken answers for core distinctions and LCEL role.
